title: Achieving strategic renewal: the multi-level influences of top and middle managers’ boundary-spanning
author: Glaser et al.

bs_measures.name: MM boundary-spanning
bs_measures.specific_measure: The MMs were presented with a list of the units and were asked to tick the units that provided them with new knowledge or expertise.
bs_measures.notes: Constructed from the number of units to which the MM in question was connected (range 1 to 30).

correlations.specific_measure: At the individual level, we controlled for gender (female =1, male =0) and job grade.

We controlled for job grade, as, within the selected group of MMs, there were two different job grades. Job grade reflects the strategically hierarchical position of a manager, serving as a proxy for power and job complexity.
The MMs were asked to indicate the extent to which they experienced role conflict by rating the items on a scale ranging from 1, “strongly disagree”, to 7, “strongly agree.”
correlations.notes: Seven items were averaged to form a single measure of role conflict after excluding one item from the Rizzo et al. (1970) eight-item scale due to cross loadings.

correlations.specific_measure: Next, we calculated the proportion of overlap for each MM, by dividing the number of mutual ties by the total number of MMs’ boundary-spanning ties.
correlations.notes: Calculated using Boolean combinations in UCINET 6, representing the proportion of overlap ranging from 0 to 1.
